explanation: This infographic features a simplified world map with only five color-coded regions, clear numeric labels, and a clean white background, resulting in very low data density and clutter. The color encoding is straightforward with five distinct hues, and the text elements are minimal and well-spaced. The cognitive load is low as the design is immediately interpretable with no specialized domain knowledge required.

This horizontal bar chart displays 19 countries with two data segments each (38 bars total), creating moderate data density. The text load is notable with 19 country labels, axis tick marks, a title, legend, subtitle, and source note, contributing to semantic complexity. However, the clean layout, simple two-color encoding, and standard chart form keep cognitive load relatively manageable, preventing a higher complexity score.

The chart contains 19 labeled categories with three stacked segments each, creating moderate data density and requiring careful reading of numerous y-axis labels. The color scheme is limited to three distinguishable colors (dark navy, purple, orange) which helps clarity, but the volume of text labels and stacked encoding adds cognitive load when comparing segments across categories. Overall layout is clean and well-organized, keeping complexity at a moderate level.

This scatter plot has moderate complexity driven primarily by the individual text labels on each of the ~16 data points, some of which cluster and overlap in the center-right region, increasing cognitive load. Four distinct colors (green, orange, purple, black) encode categorical groups without a legend, requiring inference from point labels. The overall layout is clean with minimal gridlines and standard 2D encoding, keeping complexity in the moderate range.

This grouped bar chart displays 24 bars across 12 time periods with two color-coded categories, creating moderate data density without significant clutter. The visualization includes a title, descriptive subtitle, axis labels, two annotated callouts with specific values, and source attributions, adding a moderate text load. The color scheme is simple (two colors), the layout is clean and well-organized, and the chart type requires minimal domain knowledge, keeping overall cognitive demand low.

This stacked area chart features 8 data series rendered in a narrow, similar brown/orange color palette, which increases perceptual difficulty in distinguishing individual layers. The text density is moderate, with a long two-line title, an 8-item two-row legend, and multiple axis labels with large numeric values. While the chart type is familiar and the layout is clean, the close color similarity between bands and the need to cross-reference the legend with stacked layers adds meaningful cognitive load.

This biplot-style scatter visualization uses multiple simultaneous encodings — circle size, color gradient (dark to orange), spatial position, directional arrows, and annotated vector labels — creating moderate cognitive demand. The color legend with numeric scale labels, multiple data point annotations (e.g., 'Puffed rice', 'All-Bran with Extra Fiber'), and overlapping elements in the central cluster add to perceptual load. However, the overall layout remains relatively sparse with a white background, keeping clutter manageable.

The parallel sets diagram uses crossing bands between three categorical levels, creating moderate visual clutter through overlapping teal and green flows that require tracing to interpret. Text labels are sparse and legible (6 category labels plus 3 level headers), keeping semantic complexity low. However, the overlapping and crossing flow bands require moderate cognitive effort to decode, and the low-resolution rendering adds slight perceptual difficulty.

The visualization features two densely plotted time series lines with high-frequency fluctuations spanning several years, creating moderate data density and visual noise. Multiple text elements are present including a title, two-line subtitle, axis labels, in-chart line labels, and several annotation callout boxes with specific values, adding semantic complexity. The use of multiple colors (gold, red, blue annotation boxes, yellow shading, green background) and the highlighted spread region add encoding variety, but the overall layout remains reasonably structured and interpretable.

The visualization contains numerous small elliptical marks (~200+) in three colors (red, green, blue) forming loose clusters, contributing moderate data density. However, the complete absence of text labels, axis titles, legends, and gridlines significantly reduces semantic and textual complexity. The varying orientations and sizes of the ellipses add slight perceptual noise, but the overall layout is relatively clean with recognizable cluster structure, keeping cognitive load moderate.

The visualization consists solely of concentric rings with a limited two-tone color palette (dark green and dark maroon), creating a repetitive, regular pattern with no text, labels, axes, or legend. While the density of rings increases toward the outer edges adding some perceptual complexity, the overall encoding is simple and immediately recognizable as a radial/circular pattern. The absence of any textual or symbolic elements keeps cognitive load low, resulting in a modest complexity score.

This contour/heat map plot features multiple overlapping encoding channels: a continuous color gradient (white-yellow-orange-red), dense contour lines (especially in the right region), dual x-axes and dual y-axes with different scales, two overlaid blue lines, and a legend with 11 labeled color categories. The domain-specific physics content and the dense, tightly packed contour lines in the lower-right region increase cognitive load and interpretive difficulty. The four-axis labeling system and requirement for specialized knowledge further elevate the complexity beyond a standard single-encoded chart.

The visualization presents two 3D cardiac models with multiple simultaneous encoding channels: a warm color gradient (red-orange-yellow) conveying perfusion data, overlaid grid mesh lines, and in the right model, numerous small green diamond markers densely covering the surface, increasing visual clutter significantly. The 3D rendering with perspective and specialized medical domain context (cardiac anatomy) adds schema complexity and cognitive load. Text elements are sparse and legible, but the interplay of color gradients, 3D geometry, grid patterns, and symbol overlays collectively produce moderate-to-high complexity.

This visualization layers three simultaneous encodings — a continuous color heatmap, a dense vector/arrow field, and overlaid contour lines — creating significant visual busyness and data density. The multi-channel encoding demands considerable cognitive effort to disentangle direction, magnitude, and spatial value simultaneously. While the text elements are modest (axis labels, colorbar, one annotation), the overlapping arrows and contour lines across the entire plot area substantially raise perceptual and interpretive complexity.

The visualization contains 60 small heatmap panels arranged in a 10×6 grid, creating extremely high data density and clutter. Each panel has its own internal matrix pattern requiring individual interpretation, and the column header labels (E5U, E5W, E1U, E1W combinations) demand domain knowledge to decode the pairwise comparisons. The cognitive load is very high as viewers must process both the patterns within individual panels and compare across the entire small-multiples layout.

This 3D scientific visualization exhibits extremely high data density with thousands of glyphs (oriented needles/arrows) overlaid on complex swirling flow patterns, creating intense visual clutter. Multiple simultaneous encoding channels are used — color (blue, red, yellow, green), glyph size, orientation, and 3D spatial position — requiring specialized domain knowledge to interpret the fluid/atmospheric dynamics depicted. The combination of dense overlapping elements, rich multi-hue color mapping, and high cognitive demands of parsing a perspective 3D flow field results in very high overall visual complexity.

This 3D urban visualization is extremely dense with hundreds of rectangular building blocks filling the entire frame in a complex perspective projection, creating very high data density and clutter. The grayscale shading across varied heights and orientations demands significant perceptual effort to disentangle spatial relationships, and the irregular arrangement with overlapping elements produces a chaotic, overwhelming layout. While the absence of text labels reduces one source of complexity, the sheer number of elements, 3D schema, and high cognitive load from parsing depth cues keep the score very high.

The network graph contains hundreds of nodes and edges filling nearly the entire canvas, creating high data density and significant visual clutter, especially in the densely interconnected central region. While text labels are minimal (only SG1–SG4 annotations), the tangled web of overlapping edges and clustered nodes demands considerable cognitive effort to parse structure and relationships. The layout is spatially complex with varied local topologies, pink halos on select nodes, and red circled annotations adding additional visual layers.

This simple bar chart contains only three hatched bars with red error bars, minimal axis labels, and a single title, resulting in very low data density and clutter. The visual encoding is straightforward and immediately interpretable with no specialized domain knowledge required. Text elements are sparse and legible, contributing to an overall low cognitive load.

This visualization exhibits extreme data density with thousands of individual cells packed across the entire image canvas, creating overwhelming visual clutter. The hierarchical structure transitions from large, identifiable elements at the top to nearly microscopic units at the bottom, demanding significant cognitive load to parse. The multi-scale layout combined with subtle color accents (green and cyan highlights against dense gray backgrounds) further amplifies perceptual complexity and interpretive difficulty.

This 3D flow-field visualization combines densely overlapping streamlines, multiple colored tensor glyphs (ellipsoids) of varying orientations, and scattered small directional markers throughout a three-dimensional bounding volume, creating significant data density and visual clutter. The use of multiple simultaneous encodings—3D spatial position, color (red, blue, yellow, green), shape, and orientation of glyphs alongside swirling line flow patterns—demands high cognitive load and specialized domain knowledge to interpret. While no textual labels are present (reducing one source of complexity), the perceptual challenge of disentangling overlapping 3D elements and inferring scientific meaning from the glyph-streamline interplay keeps the overall complexity score high.

This is a simple single-color bar chart with straightforward visual encoding and a clear monotonically decreasing pattern. Data density is low with roughly 20 bars and minimal graphical elements. The main complexity driver is slightly reduced text legibility due to faded axis labels and tick marks, but the overall cognitive load remains low given the familiar chart type and simple structure.

This is a straightforward dual-line chart with minimal data density — only two lines across 11 time points. The visual encoding is simple and clean with just two colors (dark and light blue), a standard legend, and clear axis labels. While there is moderate text content (title, axis labels, annotations, caption, and source), the overall layout is uncluttered and immediately interpretable with low cognitive load.

This is a straightforward single-color bar chart with moderate data density (~22 bars). The primary complexity drivers are the large number of rotated x-axis labels, several of which are long country names (e.g., 'Bolivia (Plurinational State of)', 'Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)'), which increase text legibility demands. The dual-direction bars (positive/negative) add slight cognitive load, but the overall layout is clean, uses a single color, and follows a familiar chart schema.

This is a clean donut chart with 5 clearly differentiated color segments, direct labels, and percentages — resulting in low data density and minimal clutter. The text elements (title, subtitle, segment labels, center annotation, source note) add a moderate amount of reading but are well-organized and legible. The standard chart type and straightforward color palette impose very little cognitive load on the viewer.

The stacked bar chart contains roughly 30 bars each composed of three color-coded segments, creating moderate data density with some visual clutter from the overlapping color bands. The color palette uses three distinct but somewhat hard-to-distinguish hues (blue, dark maroon, pale yellow), and the legend, axis labels, and grid lines add text and structural elements. Overall cognitive load is moderate—the chart type is familiar but requires tracking three stacked components across many categories.

The image features multiple curved text arcs with the same label repeated at progressively decreasing scales, transitioning from large legible text to nearly invisible lines. While the color palette is minimal (dark green, red dots, white background), the layered curved text paths and size variation create moderate cognitive demand in understanding the encoding concept. The repetitive nature reduces clutter somewhat, but the unusual curved-text layout and the ambiguity of purpose add moderate interpretive difficulty.

The visualization contains a dense grid of 5 columns by 16 rows with multiple colored segments per cell, requiring careful color discrimination across three shades of blue plus gray. Text density is moderate with 16 row labels, 5 multi-line column headers, and a legend, adding to cognitive load. While the grid layout is organized and relatively clean, interpreting the meaning of each colored band across many cells demands sustained visual attention and domain knowledge, placing this at moderate complexity.

The image presents two simultaneous map representations of the Netherlands—a choropleth map with numeric labels and an area-proportional tile grid—each using 10+ distinct colors, increasing both color complexity and cognitive demand. The left map contains numerous small labeled regions with overlapping text and irregular boundaries, while the right grid cartogram uses hundreds of small colored squares requiring careful comparison. The dual-encoding scheme and geographic schema demands moderate domain knowledge, raising the overall cognitive load significantly.

This stacked area chart has moderate data density with roughly 30 years of annual data across three layered categories, but the layout is clean and well-organized. The color palette is limited to three distinct shades, and text elements (title, legend, axis labels, source) are sparse and legible. The primary cognitive demand comes from interpreting stacked area layers to extract individual category values, slightly elevating complexity beyond a simple line chart.

The image contains three repeated grid-matrix charts with a simple blue color gradient encoding, minimal text (row numbers 2–6 and year labels), and a clean, repetitive layout. Data density is moderate due to the small cells across the grids, but the limited color palette and consistent structure keep cognitive load relatively low. The repetition of three similar panels adds slight complexity without introducing significant interpretive difficulty.

The visualization uses multiple simultaneous encodings — ellipse position, size, orientation, and color intensity — which elevate cognitive load and interpretive difficulty. The two blue inset zoom boxes with dashed connecting lines add structural complexity and require viewers to track spatial relationships across the image. Text labels are minimal (only the color legend), but the scattered, partially overlapping ellipses and the domain-specific nature of the glyph encoding (likely tensor or uncertainty fields) contribute to moderate-to-high perceptual demand.

The image is densely packed with approximately 50+ repeated directional glyphs arranged across a dark background, creating high data density and visual clutter. Each glyph encodes multiple data dimensions through orientation, color gradients (orange/red to green/teal/blue), and shape, requiring domain knowledge to interpret the vector-field-like encoding. While the absence of any text labels or axes reduces one dimension of complexity, the sheer number of elements with subtle per-glyph variations in color and orientation demands significant cognitive effort to parse.

The visualization presents 11 parallel timelines with multiple overlapping categorical encodings using both color (blue, red, orange-red, black, yellow) and shape (circles, triangles), requiring simultaneous decoding of 7 legend categories. Text density is moderate with 11 Y-axis user labels, axis titles, and a 7-item legend. While the layout is structured and parallel timelines aid readability, the cognitive load of tracking multiple color-shape combinations across numerous data points increases overall complexity.

The visualization is a dense hierarchical tree with numerous nodes (circles) and leaf markers (rectangles) connected by lines, creating moderate data density and structural complexity. However, the absence of any text labels, titles, or color variety (purely monochromatic grayscale) reduces semantic and color complexity significantly. The uneven layout—with dense clusters on the left and center alongside large empty white spaces on the right—creates moderate visual imbalance and requires meaningful cognitive effort to trace hierarchical relationships.

This visualization combines multiple complex visual forms — a streamgraph timeline, two large word clouds with hundreds of varying-size text items, embedded pie charts, and a multi-color legend — creating very high data density and cognitive load. The text legibility challenge is extreme due to the dense word clouds with words at many scales and orientations. Understanding the relationship between the streamgraph overview and the circular detail views requires significant interpretive effort and domain familiarity with hybrid visualization schemas.

This geographic map visualization employs multiple simultaneous encodings — color gradient (elevation trend), circle size (standard error), and varied line types (rivers, basin boundaries, glacier outlines) — creating moderate-to-high visual complexity. The data density is substantial with dozens of overlapping colored circles scattered across a textured terrain background, alongside numerous geographic labels, coordinate markers, and sub-region annotations. The legend itself is multi-part and requires domain knowledge of glaciology and the Himalayan geography to fully interpret, contributing to elevated cognitive load.

This phylogenetic cladogram combined with a geological time scale carries significant cognitive load due to specialized domain knowledge required and multiple encoding systems (branching tree structure, shaded gap regions, dashed vs. solid lines, embedded fossil image). The dense text layer at the bottom with numerous rotated geological period names substantially increases perceptual difficulty. While the overall layout is organized, the combination of cladistic branching, timeline, labeled gaps, and multi-level time period taxonomy creates moderate-to-high visual complexity.

This visualization exhibits high complexity due to extensively overlapping concentric circles in multiple colors (blue, red, orange, green, grey) covering most of the map area, creating significant visual clutter and perceptual difficulty in distinguishing individual sound zones. The legend is unusually extensive, listing both individual sound types and numerous combination categories (15+ entries), demanding considerable cognitive effort to interpret. The specialized sound-mapping schema combined with transparent overlapping elements, street labels, and dense descriptive text on the left panel substantially increases the overall cognitive load.

This oncoprint-style genomic visualization exhibits extremely high data density with dozens of patient sample columns and numerous genomic feature rows, each cell encoded with multiple colors representing different mutation types, copy number changes, and clinical variables. The semantics are highly demanding, requiring specialized domain knowledge to interpret the layered encoding scheme (red/blue for copy number gains/losses, black/orange/purple/green for mutation types, plus clinical variable encodings), with dense rotated axis labels, numerous row labels with gene names and pathway groupings, and a complex multi-category legend. The combination of high information volume, diverse color encodings, and specialized schema results in substantial cognitive load.

The visualization features an extremely dense triangular/polygonal mesh structure covering nearly the entire image area, creating high visual clutter and data density. The irregular mesh elements vary greatly in size and orientation, producing a complex geometric texture that demands significant perceptual effort to parse. While the color encoding is relatively simple (single blue hue gradient with a minimal legend), the sheer quantity of mesh elements and the domain-specific nature of the computational mesh visualization drive high cognitive load.

This visualization exhibits extreme data density with hundreds of country rows and dozens of Olympic year columns forming a massive matrix, creating severe visual clutter. Text legibility is very poor due to the tiny country name labels and year axis labels, demanding significant cognitive effort to parse. The multi-color encoding (magenta, cyan, teal/gray) across an enormous number of cells, combined with the dark background and irregular bar patterns, creates a highly complex and cognitively demanding display.

This multi-scale network visualization exhibits high data density with numerous densely packed node-link clusters, intricate edge bundles within circular groupings, and interconnecting chains of nodes across the canvas. The use of multiple colors (dark blue, red, orange, yellow), dashed circle annotations, and zoom-detail insets adds significant visual encoding complexity. Cognitive load is elevated due to the need to interpret hierarchical relationships between macro-level chains and micro-level cluster structures simultaneously, though the absence of text labels moderates the score slightly.

This visualization is extremely dense, featuring multiple coordinated views (Base Text, Overview, Page View, Line View, Word View) packed with hundreds of small colored squares, dense text labels, axis annotations, and layered visual encodings. The data density and image clutter are very high, with numerous small elements requiring significant cognitive effort to parse across multiple panels. The combination of multi-view layout, diverse color encodings (green, red, blue, yellow), abundant text at tiny scales, and specialized domain context (textual variant analysis) places this near maximum visual complexity.

This network graph contains hundreds of nodes and an extremely large number of overlapping, crossing curved edges creating dense, cluttered regions — particularly in the central-left area — which drives high data density and clutter scores. The layout has a complex 3D-like wrapping structure that increases schema and cognitive load demands. However, the strictly monochromatic palette, absence of text labels, and use of only two visual encoding channels (dots and lines) partially offset the complexity.

This visualization combines multiple complex components simultaneously: a parallel-coordinates-style flow diagram with many overlapping colored lines, numerous small matrix thumbnails on the left, a multi-category legend, and four detailed word clouds on the right where both font size and color encode meaning. The data density is extremely high, with hundreds of text elements in varying sizes and colors throughout, creating significant visual clutter and overlap. The cognitive load is very demanding as viewers must decode several distinct encoding schemes (line color/thickness, word size, word color, spatial position) across interconnected visualization types that require domain knowledge to interpret.
